is an abandoned settlement that usually contains substantial visible remaining buildings and infrastructure, such as roads. A town often becomes a ghost town because the economic activity that supported it (usually industrial or agricultural) has failed or ended for any reason (e.g. a host ore deposit exhausted by [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining")). The town may also have declined because of natural or human-caused disasters such as [floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood"), prolonged [droughts](/wiki/Drought "Drought"), government actions, war, pollution, or [nuclear and radiation-related accidents and incidents](/wiki/Nuclear_and_radiation_accidents_and_incidents "Nuclear and radiation accidents and incidents"). The term can sometimes refer to cities, towns, and neighbourhoods that, though still populated, are significantly less so than in past years; for example, those affected by high levels of unemployment and dereliction.[[1]](#cite_note-Baker-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Abandoned settlement with intact features

"Ghost city" redirects here. For archaeological deserted settlements, see [Abandoned village](/wiki/Abandoned_village "Abandoned village"). For other uses, see [Ghost city (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ghost_city_(disambiguation) "Ghost city (disambiguation)") and [Ghost town (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ghost_town_(disambiguation) "Ghost town (disambiguation)").

[![Aerial view of Plymouth, Montserrat, buried under grey volcanic ash](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Plymouth_Montserrat_Heli.jpg/500px-Plymouth_Montserrat_Heli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plymouth_Montserrat_Heli.jpg)

[Plymouth, Montserrat](/wiki/Plymouth,_Montserrat "Plymouth, Montserrat"), is the only ghost town that is the capital of a modern political [territory](/wiki/Territory "Territory"). It was rendered uninhabitable and evacuated in 1995, after being inundated with volcanic ash from the eruption of the [Soufrière Hills](/wiki/Soufri%C3%A8re_Hills "Soufrière Hills") Volcano.

A **ghost town**, **deserted city**, **extinct town**, or **abandoned city** is an abandoned settlement that usually contains substantial visible remaining buildings and infrastructure, such as roads. A town often becomes a ghost town because the economic activity that supported it (usually industrial or agricultural) has failed or ended for any reason (e.g. a host ore deposit exhausted by [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining")). The town may also have declined because of natural or human-caused disasters such as [floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood"), prolonged [droughts](/wiki/Drought "Drought"), government actions, war, pollution, or [nuclear and radiation-related accidents and incidents](/wiki/Nuclear_and_radiation_accidents_and_incidents "Nuclear and radiation accidents and incidents"). The term can sometimes refer to cities, towns, and neighbourhoods that, though still populated, are significantly less so than in past years; for example, those affected by high levels of unemployment and dereliction.[[1]](#cite_note-Baker-1)

The definition of a ghost town is debated among writers. Some restrict the term to places deserted for purely economic reasons, while others require visible remains of buildings, and still others allow for a small remaining population. Common causes of abandonment include the exhaustion of natural resources such as minerals or timber, the rerouting of railways or roads, dam construction that floods settlements, armed conflict and forced displacement, and environmental contamination. Epidemics, including the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") and the [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu"), have also depopulated communities, as has long-term exposure to hazardous materials such as [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos") and [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead").

Ghost towns are found on every continent, including Antarctica. Africa and the Middle East have towns abandoned through conflict and the collapse of empires. In Asia, settlements linked to [Gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") labour camps were deserted after the camps closed, while in Europe, [urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") has emptied villages across Bulgaria, Hungary, and Spain. The Americas contain thousands of former mining and railway towns, particularly in the western United States, British Columbia, and Chile's [Atacama Desert](/wiki/Atacama_Desert "Atacama Desert"). Australia and New Zealand have their own legacy of gold rush ghost towns.

A few ghost towns have been repopulated through [heritage tourism](/wiki/Heritage_tourism "Heritage tourism"), pilgrimage, or resettlement programmes. [Walhalla, Victoria](/wiki/Walhalla,_Victoria "Walhalla, Victoria"), in Australia revived after its gold mine closed, and [Foncebadón](/wiki/Foncebad%C3%B3n "Foncebadón") in Spain is slowly recovering owing to pilgrims on the road to [Santiago de Compostela](/wiki/Santiago_de_Compostela "Santiago de Compostela"). Some ghost towns that preserve period-specific architecture have become tourist attractions or filming locations, among them [Kolmanskop](/wiki/Kolmanskop "Kolmanskop") in Namibia, [Craco](/wiki/Craco "Craco") in Italy, [Pripyat](/wiki/Pripyat "Pripyat") in Ukraine, and [Bodie](/wiki/Bodie,_California "Bodie, California") in the United States.

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

T. Lindsey Baker, author of *Ghost Towns of Texas*, defines a ghost town as "a town for which the reason for being no longer exists."[[1]](#cite_note-Baker-1) Some writers restrict the term to settlements that were deserted because they were no longer economically viable, discounting those that were abandoned due to a natural or human-made disaster or other causes. Some believe that any settlement with visible tangible remains should not be called a ghost town;[[2]](#cite_note-Rockies-2) others say, conversely, that a ghost town should contain the tangible remains of buildings.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Whether or not the settlement must be completely deserted or may contain a small population is also a matter for debate.[[2]](#cite_note-Rockies-2) Generally, though, the term is used in a looser sense, encompassing any and all of these definitions. American author Lambert Florin defined a ghost town as "a shadowy semblance of a former self."[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Reasons for abandonment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Reasons for abandonment")]

Factors leading to the abandonment of towns include depleted [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource"), economic activity shifting elsewhere, railroads and roads bypassing or no longer accessing the town, human intervention, disasters, massacres, wars, the shifting of politics or fall of empires, and volcanic eruptions.[[5]](#cite_note-5) A town can also be abandoned if it is located within an [exclusion zone](/wiki/Exclusion_zone "Exclusion zone") due to natural or human-made causes.

### Economic decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Economic decline")]

[![Derelict farmhouse surrounded by overgrown vegetation](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Abandoned_farmhouse%2C_overgrown.jpg/250px-Abandoned_farmhouse%2C_overgrown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abandoned_farmhouse,_overgrown.jpg)

As farms [industrialise](/wiki/Intensive_farming "Intensive farming"), smaller farms are no longer economically viable, leading to rural decay.

Ghost towns may result when the single activity or resource that created a [boomtown](/wiki/Boomtown "Boomtown") (e.g., nearby mine, mill or resort) is depleted or the resource economy undergoes a "bust" (e.g., catastrophic resource price collapse). A [gold rush](/wiki/Gold_rush "Gold rush") often brought intensive but short-lived economic activity to a remote village, only to leave a ghost town once the resource was depleted.

Boomtowns can often decrease in size as quickly as they grew. Sometimes all, or nearly all, of the population can desert the town, resulting in a ghost town. The dismantling of a boomtown can often occur on a planned basis. Mining companies nowadays will create a temporary [company town](/wiki/Company_town "Company town") to service a mine site, building all the accommodations, shops and services required, and then remove them once the resource has been extracted. Modular buildings can be used to facilitate the process.

In some cases, multiple factors may remove the economic basis for a community; some former [mining towns](/wiki/Mining_town "Mining town") on [U.S. Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66") suffered both mine closures when the resources were depleted and loss of highway traffic as US 66 was diverted from places like [Oatman, Arizona](/wiki/Oatman,_Arizona "Oatman, Arizona"), onto a more direct path. Mine and [pulp mill](/wiki/Pulp_mill "Pulp mill") closures have led to many ghost towns in British Columbia, Canada, including several relatively recent ones: [Ocean Falls](/wiki/Ocean_Falls "Ocean Falls"), which closed in 1973 after the [pulp mill](/wiki/Pulp_mill "Pulp mill") was decommissioned; [Kitsault](/wiki/Kitsault "Kitsault"), whose [molybdenum](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum") mine shut down after only 18 months in 1982; and [Cassiar](/wiki/Cassiar,_British_Columbia "Cassiar, British Columbia"), whose [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos") mine operated from 1952 to 1992.

In other cases, the reason for abandonment can arise from a town's intended economic function shifting to another, nearby place. This happened to [Collingwood, Queensland](/wiki/Collingwood,_Queensland "Collingwood, Queensland"), in [Outback](/wiki/Outback "Outback") Australia when nearby [Winton](/wiki/Winton,_Queensland "Winton, Queensland") outperformed Collingwood as a regional centre for the livestock-raising industry. The railway reached Winton in 1899, linking it with the rest of [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), and Collingwood was a ghost town by the following year. More broadly across Australia, there has been a shift towards [fly-in fly-out](/wiki/Fly-in_fly-out "Fly-in fly-out") arrangements over building a [company town](/wiki/Company_town "Company town") in order to avoid the development of ghost towns once a mining resource has been fully extracted.[[6]](#cite_note-aso-6)

The Middle East (Southwest Asia) has many ghost towns and ruins that were created when the shifting of politics or the fall of empires caused capital cities to be socially or economically unviable, such as [Ctesiphon](/wiki/Ctesiphon "Ctesiphon").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The rise of real-estate speculation and the resulting possibility of [real-estate bubbles](/wiki/Real-estate_bubble "Real-estate bubble") (sometimes due to outright overbuilding by land developers) may also trigger the appearance of certain elements of a ghost town, as real-estate prices initially rise (whereupon affordable housing becomes less available) and then later fall for a variety of reasons that are often tied to economic cycles and/or marketing hubris. This has been observed in various countries, including Spain, China, the United States, and Canada, where housing is often used as an investment rather than for habitation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![Abandoned high-rise buildings along a fenced-off beachfront in Varosha](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Famagusta-Varosha_2007.JPG/500px-Famagusta-Varosha_2007.JPG)](/wiki/File:Famagusta-Varosha_2007.JPG)

Prior to the [Turkish invasion of Cyprus](/wiki/Turkish_invasion_of_Cyprus "Turkish invasion of Cyprus") in 1974, [Varosha](/wiki/Varosha,_Famagusta "Varosha, Famagusta"), now falling into ruin, was a modern tourist area.

Railroads and roads bypassing or no longer reaching a town can also create a ghost town. This was the case in many of the ghost towns along Ontario's historic [Opeongo Line](/wiki/Opeongo_Line "Opeongo Line") and along [U.S. Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66") after motorists bypassed the latter on the faster-moving highways [I-44](/wiki/Interstate_44 "Interstate 44") and [I-40](/wiki/Interstate_40 "Interstate 40"). Some ghost towns were founded along railways where [steam trains](/wiki/Steam_train "Steam train") would stop at periodic intervals for repairs or to take on water, but [dieselisation](/wiki/Dieselisation "Dieselisation") or [electrification](/wiki/Rail_electrification "Rail electrification") negated the need for the trains to stop. [Amboy, California](/wiki/Amboy,_California "Amboy, California"), was part of one such series of villages along the [Atlantic and Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Atlantic_and_Pacific_Railroad "Atlantic and Pacific Railroad") across the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert"). In other cases, railroads replaced rivers or [canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal") as the primary means of overland transport, causing the decline of towns that depended on river or canal traffic; one such town was [Granville, Indiana](/wiki/Granville,_Indiana "Granville, Indiana"), located on the [Wabash and Erie Canal](/wiki/Wabash_and_Erie_Canal "Wabash and Erie Canal").

River rerouting is another factor, one example being the towns along the [Aral Sea](/wiki/Aral_Sea "Aral Sea").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ghost towns may be created when land is [expropriated](/wiki/Eminent_domain "Eminent domain") by a government, and residents are required to relocate. One example is the village of [Tyneham](/wiki/Tyneham "Tyneham") in Dorset, England, acquired during World War II to build an artillery range.[[7]](#cite_note-wright1995-7)

A similar situation occurred in the U.S. when [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") acquired land to construct the [John C. Stennis Space Center](/wiki/John_C._Stennis_Space_Center "John C. Stennis Space Center") (SSC), a rocket testing facility in [Hancock County, Mississippi](/wiki/Hancock_County,_Mississippi "Hancock County, Mississippi") (on the Mississippi side of the [Pearl River](/wiki/Pearl_River_(Mississippi%E2%80%93Louisiana) "Pearl River (Mississippi–Louisiana)"), which is the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi")–[Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") state line). This required NASA to acquire a large (approximately 34-square-mile or 88-square-kilometre) [buffer zone](/wiki/Buffer_zone "Buffer zone") because of the loud noise and potential dangers associated with testing such rockets. Five thinly populated rural Mississippi communities (Gainesville, Logtown, Napoleon, Santa Rosa, and Westonia), plus the northern portion of a sixth ([Pearlington](/wiki/Pearlington,_Mississippi "Pearlington, Mississippi")), along with 700 families in residence, had to be completely relocated away from the facility.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![Abandoned concrete apartment buildings in a forested valley](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Akarmaraghosttown.jpg/250px-Akarmaraghosttown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Akarmaraghosttown.jpg)

[Akarmara](/wiki/Akarmara "Akarmara"), a [mining town](/wiki/Mining_town "Mining town") in Abkhazia/Georgia, was abandoned in the early 1990s due to the [War in Abkhazia](/wiki/War_in_Abkhazia_(1992%E2%80%9393) "War in Abkhazia (1992–93)").

Sometimes the town might cease to officially exist, but the physical infrastructure remains. For instance, the facility itself still contains remnants of the five Mississippi communities abandoned for the construction of SSC. These include city streets, now overgrown with forest flora and fauna, and a one-room schoolhouse. Another example of infrastructure remaining is the former town of [Weston, Illinois](/wiki/Weston,_DuPage_County,_Illinois "Weston, DuPage County, Illinois"), that voted itself out of existence and turned the land over for construction of the [Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory](/wiki/Fermilab "Fermilab"). Many houses and even a few barns remain, used for housing visiting scientists and storing maintenance equipment, while roads that used to cross through the site have been blocked off at the edges of the property with gatehouses or barricades to prevent unsupervised access.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Flooding by dams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Flooding by dams")]

The construction of dams has left ghost towns submerged. Examples include:

* [Kensico, New York](/wiki/Kensico,_New_York "Kensico, New York") was replaced by the [Kensico Reservoir](/wiki/Kensico_Reservoir "Kensico Reservoir").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* [Loyston, Tennessee](/wiki/Loyston,_Tennessee "Loyston, Tennessee"), U.S., was inundated by the creation of [Norris Dam](/wiki/Norris_Dam "Norris Dam") and reconstructed on nearby higher ground.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* [St. Thomas, Nevada](/wiki/St._Thomas,_Nevada "St. Thomas, Nevada"), U.S., flooded by up to 70 feet of water by [Lake Mead](/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead") following construction of the [Hoover Dam](/wiki/Hoover_Dam "Hoover Dam").[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* [Stiltner, West Virginia](/wiki/Stiltner,_West_Virginia "Stiltner, West Virginia"), inundated by the creation of [East Lynn Lake](/wiki/East_Lynn_Lake "East Lynn Lake") in 1969.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [The Lost Villages](/wiki/The_Lost_Villages "The Lost Villages") of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), flooded by [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway") construction in 1958.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [Nether Hambleton](/wiki/Nether_Hambleton "Nether Hambleton") and [Middle Hambleton](/wiki/Middle_Hambleton "Middle Hambleton") in Rutland, England, which were flooded to create [Rutland Water](/wiki/Rutland_Water "Rutland Water").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Ashopton](/wiki/Ashopton "Ashopton") and [Derwent](/wiki/Derwent,_Derbyshire "Derwent, Derbyshire"), England, flooded during the construction of the [Ladybower Reservoir](/wiki/Ladybower_Reservoir "Ladybower Reservoir").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* The [Tignes Dam](/wiki/Tignes_Dam "Tignes Dam") flooded the village of Tignes in France, displacing 78 families.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* [Mologa](/wiki/Mologa "Mologa") in Russia was flooded by the creation of [Rybinsk reservoir](/wiki/Rybinsk_reservoir "Rybinsk reservoir") between 1941 and 1947.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* Many ancient villages were abandoned during construction of the [Three Gorges Dam](/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam "Three Gorges Dam") in China, leading to the displacement of many rural people.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* In [Guanacaste Province](/wiki/Guanacaste_Province "Guanacaste Province"), Costa Rica, inhabitants of Arenal and Tronadora were forced to relocate in 1978 to make room for the human-made [Lake Arenal](/wiki/Lake_Arenal "Lake Arenal").[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* [Old Adaminaby](/wiki/Old_Adaminaby "Old Adaminaby") in New South Wales, Australia, was flooded by a dam of the [Snowy River Scheme](/wiki/Snowy_River_Scheme "Snowy River Scheme").[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* Construction of the [Aswan High Dam](/wiki/Aswan_Dam "Aswan Dam") on the [Nile River](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River") in Egypt submerged archaeological sites and ancient settlements, such as [Buhen](/wiki/Buhen "Buhen") under [Lake Nasser](/wiki/Lake_Nasser "Lake Nasser").[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* [Tehri](/wiki/Tehri "Tehri") was drowned after the construction of the [Tehri Dam](/wiki/Tehri_Dam "Tehri Dam") in the Indian state of [Uttarakhand](/wiki/Uttarakhand "Uttarakhand").[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* [Aceredo](/wiki/Aceredo "Aceredo") and five other villages in the region of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)"), Spain, drowned by the construction of [Alto Lindoso Dam](/wiki/Alto_Lindoso_Dam "Alto Lindoso Dam") downstream in Portugal in 1992[[24]](#cite_note-Pontevedra_2012-24) (later exposed after extreme drought conditions in early 2022[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)).
* [Capel Celyn](/wiki/Capel_Celyn "Capel Celyn"), Gwynedd, Wales, was lost to the [Trywern Flooding](/wiki/Tryweryn_flooding "Tryweryn flooding") of 1965. This was to create a reservoir, [Llyn Celyn](/wiki/Llyn_Celyn "Llyn Celyn"), in order to supply the English areas of [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") and [Wirral](/wiki/Wirral_Peninsula "Wirral Peninsula") with water for industry.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* [Dana](/wiki/Dana,_Massachusetts "Dana, Massachusetts"), [Enfield](/wiki/Enfield,_Massachusetts "Enfield, Massachusetts"), [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich,_Massachusetts "Greenwich, Massachusetts"), and [Prescott](/wiki/Prescott,_Massachusetts "Prescott, Massachusetts"), Massachusetts; four towns in the [Swift River Valley](/wiki/Quabbin%E2%80%93Swift_River_Valley "Quabbin–Swift River Valley") that were flooded in 1938 to create the [Quabbin Reservoir](/wiki/Quabbin_Reservoir "Quabbin Reservoir"), a water supply for the growing city of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and the surrounding suburbs.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Armed conflicts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Armed conflicts")]

[![Overgrown rubble and ruined buildings in Aghdam](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Aghdam_6.jpg/250px-Aghdam_6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aghdam_6.jpg)

Nature slowly reclaiming the ruins in [Aghdam](/wiki/Aghdam "Aghdam") (2010)

Some towns became deserted when their populations were [massacred](/wiki/Massacre "Massacre"), deported, or expelled. Examples include [Kayaköy](/wiki/Kayak%C3%B6y "Kayaköy"), an ancient Greek city abandoned in 1923 as a result of the [population exchange between Greece and Turkey](/wiki/Population_exchange_between_Greece_and_Turkey "Population exchange between Greece and Turkey"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) and the original French village at [Oradour-sur-Glane](/wiki/Oradour-sur-Glane "Oradour-sur-Glane") which was destroyed on [10 June 1944](/wiki/Oradour-sur-Glane_massacre "Oradour-sur-Glane massacre") when 642 of its 663 inhabitants were killed by a German [Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS") company. A new village was built after the war on a nearby site, and the ruins of the original have been maintained as a memorial.
Another example is [Aghdam](/wiki/Aghdam "Aghdam"), a city in [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"). Armenian forces occupied [Aghdam](/wiki/Aghdam "Aghdam") in July 1993 during the [First Nagorno-Karabakh War](/wiki/First_Nagorno-Karabakh_War "First Nagorno-Karabakh War"). The heavy fighting forced the entire population to flee. Upon seizing the city, Armenian forces destroyed much of the town to discourage Azerbaijanis from returning.[[30]](#cite_note-30) More damage occurred in the following decades when locals looted the abandoned town for building materials. Azerbaijan recaptured the district in November 2020 under a Russian-brokered ceasefire agreement.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[![Ruined stone buildings of the medieval village of Craco on a hilltop](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Craco_il_paese_fantasma.jpg/250px-Craco_il_paese_fantasma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Craco_il_paese_fantasma.jpg)

[Craco](/wiki/Craco "Craco"), Italy, was abandoned due to a landslide in 1963. It has since become a popular film set.

Both natural and man-made disasters can lead to the creation of ghost towns. For example, after being flooded more than 30 times since their town was founded in 1845, residents of [Pattonsburg, Missouri](/wiki/Pattonsburg,_Missouri "Pattonsburg, Missouri"), decided to relocate after two floods in 1993. With government help, the whole town was rebuilt 3 miles or 5 km away.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Craco](/wiki/Craco "Craco"), a medieval village in the Italian region of [Basilicata](/wiki/Basilicata "Basilicata"), was evacuated after a landslide in 1963. Nowadays it is a filming location for many movies, including *[The Passion of The Christ](/wiki/The_Passion_of_The_Christ "The Passion of The Christ")* by [Mel Gibson](/wiki/Mel_Gibson "Mel Gibson"), *[Christ Stopped at Eboli](/wiki/Christ_Stopped_at_Eboli_(film) "Christ Stopped at Eboli (film)")* by [Francesco Rosi](/wiki/Francesco_Rosi "Francesco Rosi"), *[The Nativity Story](/wiki/The_Nativity_Story "The Nativity Story")* by [Catherine Hardwicke](/wiki/Catherine_Hardwicke "Catherine Hardwicke") and *[Quantum of Solace](/wiki/Quantum_of_Solace "Quantum of Solace")* by [Marc Forster](/wiki/Marc_Forster "Marc Forster").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In 1984, [Centralia, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Centralia,_Pennsylvania "Centralia, Pennsylvania"), was abandoned due to an uncontainable [mine fire](/wiki/Mine_fire "Mine fire"), which began in 1962 and still rages to this day; eventually the fire reached an abandoned mine underneath the nearby town of [Byrnesville](/wiki/Byrnesville,_Pennsylvania "Byrnesville, Pennsylvania"), which caused that mine to catch on fire too and forced the evacuation of that town as well.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![Abandoned apartment blocks with overgrown trees in Pripyat](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Pripyat-today.jpg/250px-Pripyat-today.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pripyat-today.jpg)

[Pripyat](/wiki/Prypiat "Prypiat"), Ukraine, was abandoned after the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster").

Ghost towns may also occasionally come into being due to an *anticipated* [natural disaster](/wiki/Natural_disaster "Natural disaster") – for example, the Canadian town of [Lemieux, Ontario](/wiki/Lemieux,_Ontario "Lemieux, Ontario"), was abandoned in 1991 after soil testing revealed that the community was built on an unstable bed of [Leda clay](/wiki/Leda_clay "Leda clay"). Two years after the last building in Lemieux was demolished, a landslide swept part of the former townsite into the [South Nation River](/wiki/South_Nation_River "South Nation River"). Two decades earlier, the Canadian town of [Saint-Jean-Vianney](/wiki/Saint-Jean-Vianney,_Quebec "Saint-Jean-Vianney, Quebec"), Québec, also constructed on a Leda clay base, had been abandoned after a landslide on 4 May 1971, which swept away 41 homes, killing 31 people.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Following the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") of 1986, dangerously high levels of nuclear contamination escaped into the surrounding area, and nearly 200 towns and villages in Ukraine and neighbouring [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") were evacuated, including the cities of [Pripyat](/wiki/Pripyat "Pripyat") and [Chernobyl](/wiki/Chernobyl "Chernobyl"). The area was so contaminated that many of the evacuees were never permitted to return to their homes. Pripyat is the most famous of these abandoned towns; it was built for the workers of the [Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Chernobyl_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant") and had a population of almost 50,000 at the time of the disaster.[[34]](#cite_note-chernobyl-34)

#### Human health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Human health")]

[![Fenced-off abandoned buildings at Rerik West behind security barriers](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Rerik-West-September-2014.jpg/500px-Rerik-West-September-2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rerik-West-September-2014.jpg)

[Rerik West](/wiki/Rerik_West "Rerik West"), Germany. Turned into a restricted area after 1992 due to ammunition contamination from a nearby abandoned [Soviet Army](/wiki/Soviet_Ground_Forces "Soviet Ground Forces") barracks.

Significant fatality rates from epidemics have produced ghost towns. Multiple hamlets in England were depopulated in the wake of the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") in the mid-14th century. Some places in eastern [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") were abandoned after more than 7,000 Arkansans died during the [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu") epidemic of 1918 and 1919.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[35]](#cite_note-CDC-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) Several communities in Ireland, particularly in the west of the country, were wiped out due to the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") in the latter half of the 19th century and the years of economic decline that followed.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Catastrophic environmental damage caused by long-term contamination can also create a ghost town. Some notable examples are [Times Beach, Missouri](/wiki/Times_Beach,_Missouri "Times Beach, Missouri"), whose residents were exposed to a high level of [dioxins](/wiki/Dioxins_and_dioxin-like_compounds "Dioxins and dioxin-like compounds"), and [Wittenoom, Western Australia](/wiki/Wittenoom,_Western_Australia "Wittenoom, Western Australia"), which was once Australia's largest source of [blue asbestos](/wiki/Blue_asbestos "Blue asbestos"), but was shut down in 1966 due to health concerns. [Treece](/wiki/Treece,_Kansas "Treece, Kansas") and [Picher](/wiki/Picher,_Oklahoma "Picher, Oklahoma"), twin communities straddling the [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas")–[Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") border, were once one of the United States' largest sources of [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") and [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), but over a century of unregulated disposal of [mine tailings](/wiki/Mine_tailings "Mine tailings") led to groundwater contamination and [lead poisoning](/wiki/Lead_poisoning "Lead poisoning") in the town's children, eventually resulting in a mandatory [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") buyout and evacuation. Contamination due to [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition") caused by military use may also lead to the development of ghost towns. [Tyneham](/wiki/Tyneham "Tyneham"), in [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset"), was requisitioned for military exercises during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), and remains unpopulated, being littered with unexploded munitions from regular shelling.[[7]](#cite_note-wright1995-7)

## Ghost town repopulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ghost town repopulation")]

[![Walhalla township in 1910](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Walhalla_1910_view4.jpg/250px-Walhalla_1910_view4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Walhalla_1910_view4.jpg)

Walhalla township in 1910

[![Walhalla township in 2004](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/WalhallaVicCorner.jpg/250px-WalhallaVicCorner.jpg)](/wiki/File:WalhallaVicCorner.jpg)

Part of Walhalla in 2004, showing a mix of original and reconstructed buildings

[Walhalla, Victoria](/wiki/Walhalla,_Victoria "Walhalla, Victoria"), was almost abandoned after being mined for gold, but is now becoming repopulated.

A few ghost towns have managed to get a second life, and this happens for a variety of reasons.
One of these reasons is [heritage tourism](/wiki/Heritage_tourism "Heritage tourism") generating a new economy able to support residents.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

For example, [Walhalla, Victoria](/wiki/Walhalla,_Victoria "Walhalla, Victoria"), Australia, became almost deserted after its gold mine ceased operation in 1914, but owing to its accessibility and proximity to other attractive locations, it has had a recent economic and holiday population surge.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Another town, [Sungai Lembing](/wiki/Sungai_Lembing "Sungai Lembing"), Malaysia, which was almost deserted due to closure of a tin mine in 1986 was revived in 2001 and has become a tourist destination since then.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[Foncebadón](/wiki/Foncebad%C3%B3n "Foncebadón"), a village in [León](/wiki/Province_of_Le%C3%B3n "Province of León"), Spain, that was mostly abandoned and only inhabited by a mother and son, is slowly being revived owing to the ever-increasing stream of pilgrims on the road to [Santiago de Compostela](/wiki/Santiago_de_Compostela "Santiago de Compostela").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some ghost towns (e.g. [Riace](/wiki/Riace "Riace"), [Muñotello](/wiki/Mu%C3%B1otello "Muñotello")) are being repopulated by [refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") and [homeless people](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness"). In Riace, this was accomplished by a scheme funded by the Italian government which offers the housing to refugees and in Muñotello it was accomplished through an NGO ([Madrina Foundation](/w/index.php?title=Madrina_Foundation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Madrina Foundation (page does not exist)")).[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In Algeria, many cities became hamlets after the end of [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity"). They were revived with shifts in population during and after [French colonisation of Algeria](/wiki/French_Algeria "French Algeria"). [Oran](/wiki/Oran "Oran"), currently the nation's second-largest city with 1 million people, was a village of only a few thousand people before colonisation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), the second-largest city of Egypt, was a flourishing city in the ancient era but declined during the Middle Ages. It underwent a dramatic revival during the 19th century; from a population of 5,000 in 1806, it grew into a city of more than 200,000 inhabitants by 1882,[[44]](#cite_note-44) and is now home to more than four million people.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Around the world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Around the world")]

See also: [List of ghost towns by country](/wiki/List_of_ghost_towns_by_country "List of ghost towns by country")

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Africa")]

[![Sand-filled rooms inside abandoned buildings at Kolmanskop](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Kolmanskop_Ghost_Town_Buildings.jpg/250px-Kolmanskop_Ghost_Town_Buildings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kolmanskop_Ghost_Town_Buildings.jpg)

[Kolmanskop](/wiki/Kolmanskop "Kolmanskop"), Namibia (2016); a ghost town since 1956

Wars and rebellions in some African countries have left many towns and villages deserted. Since 2003, when President [François Bozizé](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boziz%C3%A9 "François Bozizé") came to power, thousands of citizens of the [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic") have been forced to flee their homes as a result of the escalating conflict between armed rebels and government troops. Villages accused of supporting the rebels, such as Beogombo Deux near [Paoua](/wiki/Paoua "Paoua"), are ransacked by government soldiers. Those who are not killed have no choice but to escape to refugee camps.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The instability in the region also leaves organised and well-equipped bandits free to terrorise the populace, often leaving villages abandoned in their wake.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Elsewhere in Africa, the town of [Lukangol](/wiki/Lukangol "Lukangol") was burnt to the ground during [tribal clashes in South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan_internal_conflict_(2011%E2%80%93present) "South Sudan internal conflict (2011–present)"). Before its destruction, the town had a population of 20,000.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The [Libyan](/wiki/Libya "Libya") town of [Tawergha](/wiki/Tawergha "Tawergha") had a population of around 25,000 before it was abandoned during the [2011 civil war](/wiki/Libyan_Civil_War_(2011) "Libyan Civil War (2011)"), and it has remained empty since. As of January 2026, [El Fasher](/wiki/El_Fasher "El Fasher"), the de-facto capital city of [Darfur](/wiki/Darfur "Darfur"), was largely abandoned, described by [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") officials as a "crime scene".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many of the ghost towns in mineral-rich Africa are former mining towns. Shortly after the start of the 1908 [diamond rush](/wiki/Diamond_rush "Diamond rush") in [German South-West Africa](/wiki/German_South-West_Africa "German South-West Africa"), now known as [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia"), the German Imperial government claimed sole mining rights by creating the *[Sperrgebiet](/wiki/Sperrgebiet "Sperrgebiet")* ("forbidden zone"),[[49]](#cite_note-Handbook-49) effectively criminalising new settlement. The small mining towns of this area, among them [Pomona](/wiki/Pomona,_Namibia "Pomona, Namibia"), [Elizabeth Bay](/wiki/Elizabeth_Bay,_Namibia "Elizabeth Bay, Namibia") and [Kolmanskop](/wiki/Kolmanskop "Kolmanskop"), were exempt from this ban, but the denial of new land claims soon rendered all of them ghost towns.

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Asia")]

[![Rows of empty high-rise residential towers in Chenggong District](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Chenggong%2C_Kunming%2C_Yunnan_province.jpg/250px-Chenggong%2C_Kunming%2C_Yunnan_province.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chenggong,_Kunming,_Yunnan_province.jpg)

Unoccupied residential complexes in the [Chenggong District](/wiki/Chenggong_District "Chenggong District"), Kunming, China

The town of [Dhanushkodi](/wiki/Dhanushkodi "Dhanushkodi"), India, is a ghost town. It was destroyed during the [1964 Rameswaram cyclone](/wiki/1964_Rameswaram_cyclone "1964 Rameswaram cyclone") and remains uninhabited in the aftermath.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Many abandoned towns and settlements in the former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") were established near [Gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") labour camps to supply necessary services. The 1950s saw the abandonment of most of these camps, which in turn led to the abandonment of the towns. One such town is located near the former Gulag camp called [Butugychag](/wiki/Butugychag "Butugychag") (also called Lower Butugychag). Other towns were deserted due to deindustrialisation and the economic crises of the early 1990s attributed to [post-Soviet conflicts](/wiki/Post-Soviet_conflicts "Post-Soviet conflicts")—one example being [Tkvarcheli](/wiki/Tkvarcheli "Tkvarcheli") in Georgia, a [coal mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining") town that suffered a drastic population decline as a result of the [War in Abkhazia](/wiki/War_in_Abkhazia_(1992%E2%80%931993) "War in Abkhazia (1992–1993)") in the early 1990s, and [Aghdam](/wiki/Aghdam "Aghdam"), made ruined and uninhabited after the [First Nagorno-Karabakh War](/wiki/First_Nagorno-Karabakh_War "First Nagorno-Karabakh War").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Although in the 2010s Chinese [ghost cities](/wiki/Underoccupied_developments_in_China "Underoccupied developments in China") became a frequent feature of discourse regarding [China's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_China "Economy of China") and [urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanization_in_China "Urbanization in China"), underoccupied cities filled up.[[51]](#cite_note-:102-51): 151 Writing in 2023, academic and former UK diplomat [Kerry Brown](/wiki/Kerry_Brown_(historian) "Kerry Brown (historian)") described the idea of Chinese ghost cities as a popular bandwagon that was shown to be a myth.[[51]](#cite_note-:102-51): 151–152

The town of [Namie](/wiki/Namie,_Fukushima "Namie, Fukushima"), along with several other towns in [Fukushima Prefecture](/wiki/Fukushima_Prefecture "Fukushima Prefecture"), Japan, was temporarily evacuated as a result of the [Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster") following the [2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2011_Tohoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami"). Following ongoing decontamination works, several portions of Namie have been fully reopened to residents, allowing reconstruction and renovation of the town's buildings to be undertaken and resettlement of the area to take place.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Europe")]

[![Ruined stone buildings lining an empty street in Oradour-sur-Glane](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Oradour-sur-Glane-Streets-1294.jpg/250px-Oradour-sur-Glane-Streets-1294.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oradour-sur-Glane-Streets-1294.jpg)

Main street of [Oradour-sur-Glane](/wiki/Oradour-sur-Glane "Oradour-sur-Glane"), France, unchanged since the German massacre

[![Weathered concrete mining building on Jussaro island](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Jussar%C3%B6_building1.jpg/250px-Jussar%C3%B6_building1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jussar%C3%B6_building1.jpg)

Abandoned mining building on the [Jussarö](/wiki/Jussar%C3%B6 "Jussarö") island in [Ekenäs](/wiki/Eken%C3%A4s,_Finland "Ekenäs, Finland"), Raseborg, Finland

[![Forest growing over the remains of abandoned wooden houses in Krivets village](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%9A%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%86.jpg/250px-%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%9A%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%86.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%9A%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%86.jpg)

The forest occupies the territory of the village of [Krivets](/w/index.php?title=Krivets,_Arkhangelsk_Oblast&action=edit&redlink=1 "Krivets, Arkhangelsk Oblast (page does not exist)") [[RU](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%9A%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%86_(%D0%90%D1%80%D1%85%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B3%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BB%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8C) "ru:Кривец (Архангельская область)")], deserted by urbanisation. [Arkhangelsk Oblast](/wiki/Arkhangelsk_Oblast "Arkhangelsk Oblast"), Russia

[Urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization")—the migration of a country's rural population into the cities—has left many European towns and villages deserted. An increasing number of settlements in Bulgaria are becoming ghost towns for this reason; at the time of the 2011 census, the country had 181 uninhabited settlements.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In Hungary, dozens of villages are also threatened with abandonment. The first village officially declared as "dead" was [Gyűrűfű](/w/index.php?title=Gy%C5%B1r%C5%B1f%C5%B1&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gyűrűfű (page does not exist)") [[hu](https://hu.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gy%C5%B1r%C5%B1f%C5%B1 "hu:Gyűrűfű")] in the late 1970s, but later it was repopulated as an [eco-village](/wiki/Eco-village "Eco-village"). Some other depopulated villages were successfully saved as small rural resorts, such as [Kán](/w/index.php?title=K%C3%A1n,_Hungary&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kán, Hungary (page does not exist)"), [Tornakápolna](/wiki/Tornak%C3%A1polna "Tornakápolna"), [Szanticska](/wiki/Szanticska "Szanticska"), [Gorica](/w/index.php?title=Gorica,_Hungary&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gorica, Hungary (page does not exist)"), and [Révfalu](/w/index.php?title=R%C3%A9vfalu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Révfalu (page does not exist)").

In Spain, large zones of the mountainous [Iberian System](/wiki/Iberian_System "Iberian System") and the [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees") have undergone heavy depopulation since the early 20th century, leaving a string of ghost towns in areas such as the [Solana Valley](/wiki/Solana_Valley "Solana Valley"). Traditional agricultural practices such as sheep and goat rearing, on which the mountain village economy was based, were not taken over by the local youth, especially after the lifestyle changes that swept over rural Spain during the second half of the 20th century. [La Estrella](/w/index.php?title=La_Estrella_(Mosqueruela)&action=edit&redlink=1 "La Estrella (Mosqueruela) (page does not exist)"), Teruel, is an 18th-century sanctuary village that became abandoned following mass [rural flight](/wiki/Rural_flight "Rural flight"). [[54]](#cite_note-Artigrama2022-54) [[55]](#cite_note-55)

Examples of ghost towns in Italy include the medieval village of [Fabbriche di Careggine](/wiki/Fabbriche_di_Careggine "Fabbriche di Careggine") near [Lago di Vagli](/wiki/Lago_di_Vagli "Lago di Vagli"), in the [province of Lucca](/wiki/Province_of_Lucca "Province of Lucca"), in [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"),[[56]](#cite_note-56) the deserted mountain village of [Craco](/wiki/Craco "Craco"), located in [Basilicata](/wiki/Basilicata "Basilicata"), which has served as a filming location,[[57]](#cite_note-57) and the ghost village of Roveraia, in the municipality of [Loro Ciuffenna](/wiki/Loro_Ciuffenna "Loro Ciuffenna"), in [province of Arezzo](/wiki/Province_of_Arezzo "Province of Arezzo"), situated near Pratovalle.[[58]](#cite_note-:0-58) The presence of the village is attested since the Middle Ages, when there was a tower.[[59]](#cite_note-59) During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") it was an important partisan base[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[58]](#cite_note-:0-58) and it was abandoned between the 1960s and the 1980s, when the last family who lived here left the village.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) Two projects have been proposed for the recovery of the village: in 2011 the proposal of Movimento Libero Perseo "Roveraia eco-lab," based on sustainability,[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) and in 2019 there was a proposal aiming to recover the village with a mix of functions called "Ecomuseum of Pratomagno."[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

[![Crumbling stone buildings amid vegetation in the abandoned village of Roveraia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Roveraia.jpg/250px-Roveraia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roveraia.jpg)

Ghost village of Roveraia

In the United Kingdom, thousands of villages were abandoned during the Middle Ages as a result of the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), revolts, and [enclosure](/wiki/Enclosure "Enclosure"), the process by which vast amounts of farmland became privately owned. Since there are rarely any visible remains of these settlements, they are not generally considered ghost towns; instead, they are referred to in archaeological circles as [deserted medieval villages](/wiki/Deserted_medieval_village "Deserted medieval village").[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Sometimes, wars and genocide end a town's life. In 1944, occupying German *[Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS")* troops murdered almost the entire population of the French village [Oradour-sur-Glane](/wiki/Oradour-sur-Glane "Oradour-sur-Glane"). A new settlement was built nearby after the war, but the old town was left depopulated on the orders of President [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle"), as a permanent memorial.[[71]](#cite_note-71) In Germany, numerous smaller towns and villages in the [former eastern territories](/wiki/Former_eastern_territories_of_Germany "Former eastern territories of Germany") were completely destroyed in the last two years of the war. These territories later became part of Poland and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), and many of the smaller settlements were never rebuilt or repopulated, for example [Kłomino](/wiki/K%C5%82omino "Kłomino") (*Westfalenhof*), [Pstrąże](/wiki/Pstr%C4%85%C5%BCe "Pstrąże") (*Pstransse*), and [Janowa Góra](/wiki/Janowa_G%C3%B3ra "Janowa Góra") (*Johannesberg*). Some villages in England were also abandoned during the war, but for different reasons. [Imber](/wiki/Imber "Imber"), on [Salisbury Plain](/wiki/Salisbury_Plain "Salisbury Plain"), and several villages in the [Stanford Battle Area](/wiki/Stanford_Battle_Area "Stanford Battle Area"), were commandeered by the [War Office](/wiki/War_Office "War Office") for use as training grounds for British and US troops. Although the occupation was intended to be a temporary measure, the residents were never allowed to return, and the villages have been used for military training ever since. Three miles or 5 km southeast of Imber is [Copehill Down](/wiki/Copehill_Down "Copehill Down"), a deserted village purpose-built for training in [urban warfare](/wiki/Urban_warfare "Urban warfare").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Disasters have also played a part in the abandonment of settlements within Europe. After the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") of 1986, [Pripyat](/wiki/Pripyat "Pripyat") and [Chernobyl](/wiki/Chernobyl "Chernobyl") were evacuated due to dangerous radiation levels; Pripyat remains completely abandoned, and Chernobyl has around 500 remaining inhabitants. [Todoque](/wiki/Todoque "Todoque") in the Canary Islands, Spain, was severely affected during the [2021 Cumbre Vieja volcanic eruption](/wiki/2021_Cumbre_Vieja_volcanic_eruption "2021 Cumbre Vieja volcanic eruption"). Hundreds of buildings were destroyed, and by 10 October new lava flows destroyed the remaining structures, leaving the town practically erased from the map.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An example in the UK of a ghost village that was abandoned before it was ever occupied is at [Polphail](/wiki/Polphail "Polphail"), [Argyll and Bute](/wiki/Argyll_and_Bute "Argyll and Bute"). The planned development of an oil rig construction facility nearby never materialised, and a village built to house the workers and their families became deserted the moment the building contractors finished their work.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: North America")]

[![Abandoned wooden hospital building in Robsart, Saskatchewan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/RobsartHospital.jpg/250px-RobsartHospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:RobsartHospital.jpg)

[Robsart Hospital](/wiki/Robsart_Hospital "Robsart Hospital"), one of many abandoned buildings in [Robsart, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Robsart,_Saskatchewan "Robsart, Saskatchewan")

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Canada")]

See also: [Lists of ghost towns in Canada](/wiki/Lists_of_ghost_towns_in_Canada "Lists of ghost towns in Canada")

[Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") has several ghost towns in parts of [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"), [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador"), and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"). Some were logging towns or dual mining and logging sites, often [developed at the behest of the company](/wiki/Company_town "Company town"). In Alberta and Saskatchewan, most ghost towns were once farming communities that have since died off due to the removal of the railway through the town or the bypass of a highway. The ghost towns in British Columbia were predominantly mining towns and prospecting camps, as well as canneries and, in one or two cases, large smelter and pulp mill towns. British Columbia has more ghost towns than any other jurisdiction on the North American continent, with more than 1,500 abandoned or semi-abandoned towns and localities.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Among the most notable are [Anyox](/wiki/Anyox "Anyox"), [Kitsault](/wiki/Kitsault "Kitsault"), and [Ocean Falls](/wiki/Ocean_Falls "Ocean Falls").

Some ghost towns have revived their economies and populations due to historical and eco-tourism, such as [Barkerville](/wiki/Barkerville,_British_Columbia "Barkerville, British Columbia"); once the largest town north of [Kamloops](/wiki/Kamloops "Kamloops"), it is now a year-round provincial museum. In Quebec, [Val-Jalbert](/wiki/Val-Jalbert,_Quebec "Val-Jalbert, Quebec") is a well-known tourist ghost town; founded in 1901 around a mechanical [pulp mill](/wiki/Pulp_mill "Pulp mill") that became obsolete when [paper mills](/wiki/Paper_mill "Paper mill") began to break down [wood fibre](/wiki/Wood_fibre "Wood fibre") by chemical means, it was abandoned when the mill closed in 1927 and reopened as a park in 1960.

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Mexico")]

A former mining town in Mexico, [Real de Catorce](/wiki/Real_de_Catorce "Real de Catorce"), has been used as a backdrop for Hollywood movies such as *[The Mexican](/wiki/The_Mexican "The Mexican")* (2001) and *[Bandidas](/wiki/Bandidas "Bandidas")* (2006).[[74]](#cite_note-74)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: United States")]

See also: [Lists of ghost towns in the United States](/wiki/Lists_of_ghost_towns_in_the_United_States "Lists of ghost towns in the United States")

[![Ruined concrete facade of the Cook Bank building in Rhyolite, Nevada](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/A363%2C_Rhyolite%2C_Nevada%2C_USA%2C_John_S_Cook_and_Company_building%2C_2004.jpg/250px-A363%2C_Rhyolite%2C_Nevada%2C_USA%2C_John_S_Cook_and_Company_building%2C_2004.jpg)](/wiki/File:A363,_Rhyolite,_Nevada,_USA,_John_S_Cook_and_Company_building,_2004.jpg)

Cook Bank building in [Rhyolite, Nevada](/wiki/Rhyolite,_Nevada "Rhyolite, Nevada"), a gold mining town

Many ghost towns or abandoned [communities](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") exist in the American [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"), the rural areas of which have lost a third of their population since 1920. Thousands of communities in the northern plains states of [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), and [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") became railroad ghost towns when a rail line failed to materialise. Hundreds of towns were abandoned as the [Interstate highway system](/wiki/Interstate_highway_system "Interstate highway system") replaced the railroads as the favoured means of transportation. Ghost towns are common in mining or [mill towns](/wiki/Mill_town "Mill town") in all the western states and many eastern and southern states as well. Residents are compelled to leave in search of more productive areas when the resources that had created an employment boom in these towns are eventually exhausted.

Sometimes a ghost town consists of many abandoned buildings as in [Bodie, California](/wiki/Bodie,_California "Bodie, California"), or standing ruins as in [Rhyolite, Nevada](/wiki/Rhyolite,_Nevada "Rhyolite, Nevada"), while elsewhere only the foundations of former buildings remain as in [Graysonia, Arkansas](/wiki/Graysonia,_Arkansas "Graysonia, Arkansas"). Old mining camps that have lost most of their population at some stage of their history, such as [Aspen](/wiki/Aspen,_Colorado "Aspen, Colorado"), [Deadwood](/wiki/Deadwood,_South_Dakota "Deadwood, South Dakota"), [Oatman](/wiki/Oatman,_Arizona "Oatman, Arizona"), [Tombstone](/wiki/Tombstone,_Arizona "Tombstone, Arizona") and [Virginia City](/wiki/Virginia_City,_Montana "Virginia City, Montana") are sometimes referred to as ghost towns, although they are presently active towns and cities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Many U.S. ghost towns, such as [South Pass City in Wyoming](/wiki/South_Pass_City,_Wyoming "South Pass City, Wyoming") are listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-nris-76)

[![Stone assay office building at Vulture Mine in Arizona](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Wickenburg_Vulture_Mine-Assay_office-1884-1.jpg/250px-Wickenburg_Vulture_Mine-Assay_office-1884-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wickenburg_Vulture_Mine-Assay_office-1884-1.jpg)

1884 [assay office](/wiki/Assay_office "Assay office") in [Vulture City, Arizona](/wiki/Vulture_City,_Arizona "Vulture City, Arizona"), a gold mining town

Starting in 2002, an attempt to declare an official ghost town in California stalled when the adherents of the town of [Bodie](/wiki/Bodie,_California "Bodie, California") and those of [Calico](/wiki/Calico,_San_Bernardino_County,_California "Calico, San Bernardino County, California"), in [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"), could not agree on the most deserving settlement for the recognition. A compromise was eventually reached—Bodie was designated the official state [gold rush](/wiki/Gold_rush "Gold rush") ghost town in 2002, while Calico was named the official state [silver rush](/wiki/Silver_rush "Silver rush") ghost town in 2005.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: South America")]

In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, a wave of European immigrants arrived in Brazil and settled in the cities, which offered jobs, education, and other opportunities that enabled newcomers to enter the middle class. Many also settled in the growing small towns along the expanding railway system. Since the 1930s, many rural workers have moved to the big cities. Other ghost towns were created in the aftermath of dinosaur fossil rushes.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

In [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), a [volcano erupted](/wiki/Armero_tragedy "Armero tragedy") in 1985, and the city of Armero was engulfed by lahars, which killed approximately 23,000 people in total.[[79]](#cite_note-79) Armero was never rebuilt (its inhabitants being diverted to nearby cities, and thus becoming a ghost town), but still stands today as "holy land," as dictated by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Several ghost towns throughout South America were once mining camps or lumber mills, such as the many [saltpeter](/wiki/Sodium_nitrate "Sodium nitrate") mining camps that prospered in Chile from the end of the [Saltpeter War](/wiki/War_of_the_Pacific "War of the Pacific") until the invention of synthetic saltpeter during World War I. Some of these towns, such as the [Humberstone and Santa Laura Saltpeter Works](/wiki/Humberstone_and_Santa_Laura_Saltpeter_Works "Humberstone and Santa Laura Saltpeter Works") in the [Atacama Desert](/wiki/Atacama_Desert "Atacama Desert"), have been declared [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Oceania")]

[![Stone ruins of buildings in the arid landscape of Farina, South Australia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Ruins_at_Farina.JPG/250px-Ruins_at_Farina.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ruins_at_Farina.JPG)

After many years of drought and dust storms, the town of [Farina, South Australia](/wiki/Farina,_South_Australia "Farina, South Australia"), was abandoned.

The boom and bust of gold rushes and the mining of other ores have led to a number of ghost towns in both Australia and New Zealand. Other towns have become abandoned, whether due to natural disasters, the weather, or the drowning of valleys to increase the size of lakes.

In Australia, the [Victoria gold rush](/wiki/Victoria_gold_rush "Victoria gold rush") led to numerous ghost towns (such as [Cassilis](/wiki/Cassilis,_Victoria "Cassilis, Victoria") and [Moliagul](/wiki/Moliagul "Moliagul")), as did the hunt for gold in [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") (for example, the towns of [Ora Banda](/wiki/Ora_Banda "Ora Banda") and [Kanowna](/wiki/Kanowna,_Western_Australia "Kanowna, Western Australia")). The mining of iron and other ores has also led to towns flourishing briefly before dwindling. The town of [Wittenoom](/wiki/Wittenoom,_Western_Australia "Wittenoom, Western Australia") was abandoned and demolished due to the health hazards posed by [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos") mining in the area.

In New Zealand, the [Otago gold rush](/wiki/Otago_gold_rush "Otago gold rush") similarly led to several ghost towns (such as [Macetown](/wiki/Macetown "Macetown")).[[82]](#cite_note-82) New Zealand's ghost towns also include numerous coal mining areas in the South Island's [West Coast Region](/wiki/West_Coast,_New_Zealand "West Coast, New Zealand"), including [Denniston](/wiki/Denniston,_New_Zealand "Denniston, New Zealand") and [Stockton](/wiki/Stockton,_New_Zealand "Stockton, New Zealand").[[83]](#cite_note-83) Natural disasters have also led to the loss of some towns, notably [Te Wairoa](/wiki/Te_Wairoa_(village) "Te Wairoa (village)"), "The Buried Village," destroyed in the [1886 eruption of Mount Tarawera](/wiki/1886_eruption_of_Mount_Tarawera "1886 eruption of Mount Tarawera"),[[84]](#cite_note-84) and the Otago town of [Kelso](/wiki/Kelso,_New_Zealand "Kelso, New Zealand"), abandoned after it was flooded repeatedly after heavy rainstorms. Early settlements on the rugged southwest coast of the South Island at [Martins Bay](/wiki/Martins_Bay "Martins Bay") and [Port Craig](/wiki/Port_Craig "Port Craig") were also abandoned, mainly due to the inhospitable terrain.

### Antarctica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghost_town&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Antarctica")]

[![Rusted and collapsed buildings of the former British base at Whalers Bay, Deception Island](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Deception-Base.jpg/250px-Deception-Base.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deception-Base.jpg)

The derelict British base in Whalers Bay, [Deception Island](/wiki/Deception_Island "Deception Island"), destroyed by a volcanic eruption

The oldest ghost town in [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") is on [Deception Island](/wiki/Deception_Island "Deception Island"), where in 1906, a Norwegian-Chilean company set up a whaling station at Whalers Bay, which they used as a base for their factory ship, the *Gobernador Bories*. Other whaling operations followed suit, and by 1914 there were thirteen factory ships based there. The station ceased to be profitable during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), and was abandoned in 1931. A volcanic eruption partially destroyed the station in 1969. There are also many abandoned scientific and military bases in Antarctica, especially in the [Antarctic Peninsula](/wiki/Antarctic_Peninsula "Antarctic Peninsula").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

The Antarctic island of [South Georgia](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_the_South_Sandwich_Islands "South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands") had several active whaling settlements during the first half of the 20th century, with a combined population exceeding 2,000 in some years. These included [Grytviken](/wiki/Grytviken "Grytviken") (operating 1904–64), [Leith Harbour](/wiki/Leith_Harbour "Leith Harbour") (1909–65), [Ocean Harbour](/wiki/Ocean_Harbour "Ocean Harbour") (1909–20), [Husvik](/wiki/Husvik "Husvik") (1910–60), [Stromness](/wiki/Stromness_(South_Georgia) "Stromness (South Georgia)") (1912–61), and [Prince Olav Harbour](/wiki/Prince_Olav_Harbour "Prince Olav Harbour") (1917–34). The abandoned settlements have become increasingly dilapidated and remain uninhabited nowadays except for the Museum curator's family at [Grytviken](/wiki/Grytviken "Grytviken"). The jetty, the church, dwellings, and industrial buildings at [Grytviken](/wiki/Grytviken "Grytviken") have recently been renovated by the South Georgian government, becoming a popular tourist destination. Some historical buildings in the other settlements are being restored as well.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)
